skill.name: current-location-weather
skill.description: Get current weather conditions for an IP-detected location or a specified city using public weather services.
skill.summary: Checks current weather for a detected or specified location.
skill.author: DanielMax937

security_audit.summary: The confirmed risk is external network access for IP-based geolocation and weather lookup. Static high-risk weak-crypto and obfuscation findings were false positives after context review, and no prompt injection attempt was found.

medium_findings.title: Automatic IP Geolocation Request
medium_findings.description: The script contacts ip-api.com over plain HTTP when no location is provided. This exposes the runner's IP-derived location to a third party and allows network observers to inspect or modify the response.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly calls http://ip-api.com/json/ before requesting weather data. The behavior is documented and legitimate, but it has clear privacy and transport-security implications.

medium_findings.title: External Weather Service Request
medium_findings.description: The script sends the selected or detected location to wttr.in. This is necessary for the skill, but users should understand that location queries leave the local environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are constructed from the location value and opened with urllib. This confirms third-party network access without showing malicious exfiltration intent.

low_findings.title: Documented Command Examples
low_findings.description: SKILL.md includes shell examples for running the Python script. These examples are expected usage instructions, but users should review community scripts before execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The backtick detections are Markdown code fences and example commands, not dynamic command execution inside the skill. The underlying script still requires user-authorized execution.

low_findings.title: Static High-Risk Pattern False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and obfuscation alerts do not correspond to cryptographic calls, encoded payloads, or hidden execution logic in the reviewed lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain front matter, a shebang, weather field names, and argparse description settings. No weak crypto algorithm use or obfuscated code was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Plain HTTP Third-Party Request
dangerous_patterns.description: The geolocation request uses HTTP instead of HTTPS. This can expose request metadata and allows response tampering on untrusted networks.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URL literal starts with http:// and is passed directly to urlopen. The risk is transport security, not confirmed malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded External Endpoints
dangerous_patterns.description: The script depends on ip-api.com and wttr.in. This creates privacy and availability risks outside the user's control.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The endpoints are visible string literals used for the skill's core behavior. The code does not hide the destinations or send unrelated local data.

content.value_statement: Users need quick weather context without opening a separate weather site. This skill runs a small Python utility to fetch current conditions for a detected or named location.
actual_capabilities: Detects an approximate location from the runner's public IP address.
Fetches current weather conditions from wttr.in.
Accepts a specific city or place through a location argument.
Returns compact text output for quick reading.
Returns structured weather details when JSON format is requested.
limitations: Requires network access to external geolocation and weather services.
IP-based location can be inaccurate or affected by VPNs.
Does not provide private, official, or guaranteed weather data.
Weather availability depends on ip-api.com and wttr.in uptime.

use_cases.title: Quick Local Check
use_cases.description: Ask for current conditions near the active environment before planning errands or travel.

use_cases.title: Travel Location Lookup
use_cases.description: Check weather for a named city before packing, commuting, or choosing a route.

use_cases.title: Automation-Friendly Weather Output
use_cases.description: Request structured current weather details for use in scripts or assistant workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Check My Weather
prompt_templates.prompt: What is the current weather for my detected location?
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request using automatic IP-based location detection.

prompt_templates.title: Check a City
prompt_templates.prompt: What is the current weather in Tokyo, Japan?
prompt_templates.scenario: Simple lookup for a specific place.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Packing Needs
prompt_templates.prompt: Check the current weather in London and tell me if I should carry a jacket or umbrella.
prompt_templates.scenario: Practical advice based on current conditions.

prompt_templates.title: Get Structured Conditions
prompt_templates.prompt: Get current weather for Paris in structured form and summarize temperature, wind, humidity, and UV index.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for detailed fields and a concise summary.

output_examples.input: What is the weather near me?
output_examples.output: Detected location: San Francisco, United States. Current conditions are partly cloudy, 15 degrees Celsius, 72 percent humidity, and light southwest wind.
New York currently has clear conditions with the temperature, humidity, and wind speed reported from the weather service.

output_examples.input: Should I bring an umbrella in London?
output_examples.output: The assistant checks current London conditions and gives a short recommendation based on rain, cloud cover, and wind.

best_practices: Use a specific location when accuracy matters.
Tell users that IP-based lookup contacts third-party services.
Use structured output when another tool needs to parse the result.
anti_patterns: Do not treat IP-based location as exact.
Do not use this skill for emergency weather alerts.
Do not run it when third-party location sharing is not acceptable.

faq.question: Does this skill need an API key?
faq.answer: No. It uses public services that do not require an API key.

faq.question: How does automatic location detection work?
faq.answer: The script contacts an IP geolocation service to estimate the current city.

faq.question: Can I choose a specific location?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide a city or place name instead of using automatic detection.

faq.question: Does it provide forecasts?
faq.answer: The implementation focuses on current conditions, although the provider may return more data in structured output.

faq.question: What data leaves my environment?
faq.answer: Your public IP is visible to the geolocation service, and location queries are sent to the weather service.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a network and privacy warning because it contacts third-party services.
